# [American Economic Association](/)

![American Economic Association](/assets/6b05f9b7/images/logo.svg)

Research Highlights Article

September 21, 2021

# Patterns of US migration

## How do social networks influence where migrants choose to settle?

[Tyler Smith](mailto:research.highlights@aeapubs.org)

![](/assets/6b05f9b7/images/x-logo-black.png)
![](/assets/6b05f9b7/images/bluesky_logo.svg)
![](/assets/6b05f9b7/images/fb_icon.svg)
![](/assets/6b05f9b7/images/email_icon.svg)
![](/content/file?id=15281)

Black southerners fanned out across the US during the Great Migration.

Source: Farm Security Administration - Office of War Information Photograph Collection (Library of Congress)

Two mass migrations in the mid-twentieth century significantly reshuffled the population of the United States. 

The first, starting in roughly 1910 and lasting until the 1970s, saw nearly 6 million Black Americans [leave](https://www.historians.org/teaching-and-learning/teaching-resources-for-historians/teaching-and-learning-in-the-digital-age/the-history-of-the-americas/migration-and-the-american-south/migration-and-the-twentieth-century-south-an-overview) the South to avoid persecution and find a better life. The second was the [depopulation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Depopulation_of_the_Great_Plains) of the Great Plains, spurred by the Dust Bowl years of the 1930s. 

Both groups of migrants fanned out across the United States, but the cities and towns where they ultimately settled were intimately tied to the family, friends, and acquaintances that went before them, according to [a paper in the *American Economic Journal: Applied Economics*](https://www.aeaweb.org/articles?id=10.1257/app.20180294). 

“Social networks play an important role in helping individuals figure out where to move,” author [Bryan A. Stuart](https://www.bryan-stuart.com/) told the AEA in an interview. “A lot of the role of networks happens through the transmission of information—basically helping migrants find good opportunities that would be hard to find otherwise.”

Stuart and coauthor [Evan J. Taylor](https://sites.google.com/site/evanjtaylor/) used these two historic migration episodes to determine the extent to which social networks influence location decisions.

Migrant experiences in the United States varied widely in the twentieth century, but historical accounts suggest that migration networks frequently played a role. The [story](https://www.aahsb.com/featuredstories/rok0l9a55wmd8mjxrb8qxiqa8okju1) of John McCord and the migrant connection he helped establish between two American cities illustrates a common pattern.

**If you're a policymaker and you're trying to help relocate people, it's important that you think about what networks people have and what location choices people in those networks have made.**

**Bryan A. Stuart**

When McCord was nineteen years old, he traveled north from his hometown Pontotoc, Mississippi, in search of higher wages. In 1914, McCord eventually settled in Beloit, Wisconsin, where he got a job at a manufacturing company.

After two years in Beloit, McCord went back home for a vacation and returned with 18 men, all of whom were soon hired at the same company. Thus began a persistent flow of Americans from Pontotoc to Beloit. In fact, by the time individuals born from 1916 to 1936 had reached old age, 14 percent of migrants from Pontotoc lived in Beloit’s county.

A similar pattern occurred with other city pairs across the United States in the decades after McCord and his acquaintances settled in Beloit. 

To capture these links, the researchers analyzed [administrative records](https://www.openicpsr.org/openicpsr/project/118301/version/V1/view) that contained information about the birth town and old-age residence of over 70 million Americans. Using this data, they summarized the strength of birth town networks in a single index.

“We came up with a measure of the importance of networks for basically every receiving county [in the United States],” Stuart said. “I think that granularity helps us uncover new patterns and provides more light on the underlying mechanisms here.”

The measure compared whether people born in the same town were more likely to live in the same destination in old age than people born in nearby towns. Such an approach, the authors argue, distinguishes the effect of birth town social networks from other factors such as wages, moving costs, and regional amenities.

![](/content/file?id=15279)

 

They found that when one African American moved from a Southern birth town to a destination county, 1.9 additional Black migrants made the same move on average. For White migrants from the Great Plains, the average was only 0.4.

The authors’ findings shed light on an important period of migration in the United States, and may provide a guide for lawmakers today, according to Stuart.

“If you're a policymaker and you're trying to help relocate people, it's important that you think about what networks people have and what location choices people in those networks have made,” Stuart said.

♦

“[Migration Networks and Location Decisions: Evidence from US Mass Migration](https://www.aeaweb.org/articles?id=10.1257/app.20180294)” appears in the July 2021 issue of the *[American Economic Journal: Applied Economics](https://www.aeaweb.org/journals/app).*

--- SOURCE 2: World migration patterns ---
URL: https://www.ebsco.com/research-starters/anthropology/world-migration-patterns

SUMMARY:
During Weiner’s three subsequent eras of twentieth century global migration, the United States continued to become an ever more ethnically diverse country of immigrants, and Europe itself was profoundly reshaped by **immigrant** flows. The era of forced migration of African slaves came to its conclusion in midcentury, but between 1820 and 1914 more than 44 million Europeans voluntarily migrated to the United States alone—a total number 6 million people higher than the total population of France at the time when it was the most populous country in western Europe. Meanwhile, under the terms of the Potsdam Agreement signed by the principal victors in World War II in 1945, massive, largely forced migrations had already occurred within Europe, when approximately 16 million ethnic Germans were sent west from Czechoslovakia and other Eastern European countries.

FULL CONTENT:
[Skip to main navigation](#block-cog-ebsco-main-menu)

[Research Starters Home](/research-starters)

# World migration patterns

World migration patterns have evolved significantly over centuries, influenced by historical, political, and economic factors. Myron Weiner's examination of global migration identifies five distinct eras, with the United States and Europe playing pivotal roles. The initial waves of migration, commencing from the sixteenth century, involved European colonization and the establishment of settlements in the Americas, Australia, and New Zealand. This era saw considerable voluntary migration from European countries as well as the forced migration of Africans through the slave trade.

As the twentieth century progressed, migration patterns shifted, with increasing diversity in the ethnic composition of both the U.S. and Europe due to various conflicts and economic demands. Post-World War II reconstruction in Europe resulted in labor shortages, prompting countries to recruit workers from former colonies and developing nations. This led to a rise in immigration from culturally distinct regions, altering demographic landscapes.

Currently, the United States remains a primary destination for migrants, hosting nearly 51 million foreign-born residents as of 2020. Globally, approximately 281 million individuals reside outside their home countries, with significant proportions in Europe and Asia. Contemporary migration continues to be shaped by globalization, economic opportunities, and social dynamics, making it a crucial topic for understanding modern societies.

**Published in**: 2023

**By**: Rudolph, Joseph R., Jr.

[Go to EBSCOhost](https://openurl.ebsco.com/results?sid=ebsco%3Aebsco.com%3ARS&bquery=World+migration+patterns&link_origin=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.ebsco.com) and sign in to access more content about this topic.

## Subject Terms

* [Human migration patterns](https://openurl.ebsco.com/results?sid=ebsco%3Aebsco.com%3ARS&bquery=Human%20migration%20patterns&link_origin=https%3A//www.ebsco.com)

# World migration patterns

**Definition:** Trends and patterns in international [human migration](/research-starters/environmental-sciences/human-migration) in modern world history

**Significance:***Although the United States has had a unique immigration history, many elements of immigration into the country fit into broad patterns affecting other parts of the world, particularly western Europe. US immigration history cannot, therefore, be fully understood without reference to its place in a global context.*

In his 1995 study of global migration, Myron Weiner identified five distinct eras of immigration that had occurred since the Renaissance. The United States featured centrally in each, although perhaps less so than Europe. The first two eras stretched from the seventeenth century through [World War I](/research-starters/diplomacy-and-international-relations/world-war-i) (1914–1918). Those eras encompassed Europe’s acquisition of colonies in the Americas, Australia, and New Zealand during the seventeenth, eighteenth, and very early nineteenth centuries and the settlement of those colonies through the nineteenth century. North America in general and the United States in particular benefited from the arrival of immigrants from Great Britain, France, Ireland, Germany, Italy, and their neighboring "donor" states in southern and eastern Europe.

The same eras also saw European governments shape the travel of non-Europeans to distant regions, most aggressively through the [slave trade](/research-starters/law/slave-trade-united-states). During Weiner’s three subsequent eras of twentieth century global migration, the United States continued to become an ever more ethnically diverse country of immigrants, and Europe itself was profoundly reshaped by **immigrant** flows. These eras continue to be studied to reveal how human migration has evolved over time and in response to factors such as war, political unrest, and agricultural and industrial development, and such an analytical framework provides insight into contemporary and future trends in world migration as well.

## The Age of Colonialism

History, and the spread of humanity across the globe, is to no small extent the story of ancient migrations. However, Weiner and most modern historians and sociologists date the modern waves of migration from those of the sixteenth to eighteenth centuries, when European governments in their search for wealth and power sent their peoples to remote lands, first to find goods of value and then to secure their holdings with settlements or colonies, a process that became known as [colonialism](/research-starters/anthropology/colonialism). Most of these early settlements were merely coastal affairs, under the protective cannons of their homeland’s warships. European firearms were primitive by the standards that would develop during the nineteenth century, but even the single-shot, muzzle-loading weapons that earlier empire builders brought with them from Europe were superior to most weapons of the native peoples whom they encountered in the Americas, Australasia, Asia, and Africa, and the numbers in which they eventually arrived were overwhelming to indigenous populations. However, neither their numbers nor their weaponry were great enough to enable them to move far from their initial settlements and the protective cover they enjoyed there.

The numbers of settlers involved during the long initial era of modern migration were not insignificant by the European standards of the time. Before 1820, an estimated 240,000 Europeans migrated to North America alone. Some came as **indentured servants**, and many sought to escape the religious and political persecution they experienced in Europe. To these must be added the large number of forced immigrants who were imported into North America from Africa during the slave trade that emerged during this period. Above all, perhaps, this early wave of migration established the three patterns that were to characterize international migration into the twentieth century.

First, the flow of migrants was largely unrestricted until the consolidations of European borders during the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries would cut down on movements of groups within Europe. During the nineteenth century, several countries in the Americas would pass laws prohibiting the entry of the diseased and other people seen as undesirable, such as the [Roma](/research-starters/ethnic-and-cultural-studies/roma), at the same time that European states were trying to expel them. Nevertheless, for the most part, transoceanic movements of peoples were unaffected by laws restricting immigration.

Secondly, despite some European settlements that were established in the Southern Hemisphere (and eventually the heavy flow of enslaved Africans to Brazil and other parts of South America), the most influential human migrations for Western society were essentially north-north movements, occurring within the Northern Hemisphere across an east-west axis—most frequently, from Old World Europe to the receiving areas of the New World of North America.

Finally, the arrival of immigrant European communities frequently displaced the native populations. The latter, in turn, often found it expedient to move deeper into the interior of their lands or were forced to do so.

## First Modern Era of Mass Migration

These patterns permeated global migrations through the nineteenth century. The era of forced migration of African slaves came to its conclusion in midcentury, but between 1820 and 1914 more than 44 million Europeans voluntarily migrated to the United States alone—a total number 6 million people higher than the total population of France at the time when it was the most populous country in western Europe. An increasing number of people emigrated from Asia to the New World, but this movement was still largely within the globe’s Northern Hemispheric corridors. With the arrival of so many newcomers in the Americas, native populations shrank, not only as percentages of the total populations but also in absolute numbers, as they perished from alien diseases and were often pushed into less hospitable parts of continents they once controlled. In the United States, many Native Americans were shoved westward into what was called [Indian Territory](/research-starters/social-sciences-and-humanities/indian-territory) during the 1830s, ahead of incoming settlers from Europe; others were confined to reservations during the 1880s.

The factors accounting for this mass wave of migration were both sociopolitical and scientific-technological. The middle of the nineteenth century was a particularly turbulent time in Europe, as whole societies were shaken by the revolutions of 1832 and 1848. Large numbers of people began to consider journeying to the Americas to seek better lives and greater freedom. Meanwhile, reforms introduced in Ireland by the British government combined in midcentury with a fungal infection in the potato crop to produce the [Great Famine](/research-starters/history/great-irish-famine) in Ireland between 1845–1852, and the beginnings of a mass flight of hundreds of thousands from that island to the United States.

Technological changes also helped further this movement. The emergence of steam-powered ships made the transportation of large numbers of people from Europe and Asia to American ports cheaper, safer, and above all swifter. They cut travel time for transatlantic voyages from as many as five weeks in 1700 to fewer than eight days by the advent of World War I (1914–1918). Likewise, the development of transcontinental railroads in the United States after the Civil War provided a means of moving large numbers into the American Midwest and eventually the Far West, where land was plentiful. Industrializing cities needed laborers, and federal troops could provide safety from hostile threats on the frontier by availing themselves of rapidly evolving weapon technologies. In 1840, breech-loading rifles revolutionized combat by replacing muzzle-loading muskets, which for two centuries had limited soldiers to firing only one round per minute, while requiring them to stand and expose themselves to enemy fire while reloading. With breech loaders, soldiers or settlers could fire seven rounds per minute from behind cover, and the rifles were more powerful and more accurate than muskets. By the time of the Civil War, Gatling guns raised firing rates to more than one hundred rounds per minute. It was not a coincidence that shortly afterward [Indian wars](/research-starters/military-history-and-science/american-indian-wars) came to their end in North America.

The product of all these developments was a growing tide of migration to the Americas that transformed the US population from its overwhelming British-based stock at the time of independence into the multiethnic and multiracial mixture out of which the multicultural American nation would emerge in the twentieth century. Indeed, the influx reached its peak during the early years of the twentieth century, when approximately 3 million immigrants entered the United States every year. The number would have been even higher, had not the unification of Germany between 1860 and 1870, and the resultant growth there of pride and identification with Imperial Germany, slowed significantly what had previously been a major point of origin of migration to the United States.

Finally, scientific discoveries and technological changes had another noteworthy effect during the second half of the nineteenth century that would profoundly affect the nature of global migration a century later. Simply stated, these developments helped lead to the age of world empires. The revolutionary changes in weaponry allowed European forces to relatively easily subdue native peoples armed only with spears, arrows, bladed weapons, and occasionally obsolete firearms (though in many cases such peoples put up stiff resistance that surprised the better-equipped Europeans). Moreover, the emergence of steam-powered riverboats allowed Europeans to carry their weapons deep into the interiors of regions where previously only coastal areas could be protected. The discovery of quinine as a preventative against malaria allowed Europeans to penetrate tropical regions with protection from a disease that previously was often a greater threat to European armies than native forces.

Meanwhile, the invention and development of the telegraph and telephone, coupled with the laying of transoceanic cables, allowed heads of states, their generals, and field commanders to be in constant communication. Packaged for empire building, these discoveries, technologies, and inventions changed the nature of politics around the world, and the portion of the earth’s land surfaces peopled by Europeans or under European control jumped from approximately 30 percent in 1820 to more than 80 percent by the time of World War I. By the start of the next world war in 1939, increasing numbers of the non-European subjects of these empires would begin their own south-to-north migrations to the capitals of their imperial rulers.

## World War I Through the Cold War

The shift from a predominantly north-to-north to a largely south-to-north pattern of immigration was not the only change involving patterns of global migration during the early years of the twentieth century. The door to largely unrestricted entry into the United States that was beginning to close slowly near the end of the nineteenth century was nearly completely shut. The process began with the passage of US laws forbidding the admission of the Chinese (the [Chinese Exclusion Act](/research-starters/history/chinese-exclusion-act)) and the insane. After President [William McKinley](/research-starters/military-history-and-science/william-mckinley) was assassinated in 1901, anarchists were excluded from the country. A 1917 [immigration law](/research-starters/law/immigration-law) required literacy tests. In 1921, a **quota system** was imposed on immigrants from individual countries, based on the percentages of people from each country who had been resident in the United States in 1910. Three years later, a new law made the quota system even more restrictive by pushing the baseline year back to 1890—a time when very few eastern and southern [European immigrants](/research-starters/social-sciences-and-humanities/european-immigrants) had been in the country. The quota laws naturally favored future immigrants from northern and western Europe, but they also sharply curtailed the total numbers of immigrants permitted to enter the country legally. Meanwhile, by the first decades of the twentieth century, aboriginal peoples of North America and Australia no longer had to be relocated to make room for new immigrants from abroad. The native peoples still trying to live in traditional ways had long since been moved to Australia’s Outback and to tribal [reservations in the United States](/research-starters/politics-and-government/reservation-system-united-states) and [reserves in Canada](/research-starters/politics-and-government/reserve-system-canada).

Even without the enactment of US quota restrictions, immigration from Europe to the United States would likely have slackened considerably during the years between the end of World War I and the commencement of [World War II](/research-starters/military-history-and-science/world-war-ii). Wartime dislocations, a plague that traveled through Europe after World War I, and the deprivations wrought by the [Great Depression](/research-starters/history/halifax-explosion) contributed to widespread poverty that left few Europeans with sufficient resources to afford transatlantic passage at the same moment the Great Depression was raising unemployment levels to unprecedented heights the United States. During some Depression years, the United States actually experienced negative net immigration.

Meanwhile, within Europe and neighboring regions, the revolutions of 1917 and subsequent civil war in Russia and the collapse of the **Ottoman Empire** following World War I produced significant population movements within Europe. An estimated 3 million Russians, Poles, and Germans left the collapsing Russian Empire amid its political unrest. Significant numbers of Russian nationals relocated to France. Meanwhile, in the Balkans, Muslims moved in sizeable numbers to Turkey. Likewise, the postwar decision of the newly created **League of Nations** to [make Palestine a homeland destination for Jewish people](/research-starters/history/league-nations-establishes-mandate-palestine) led to the movement of approximately 400,000 European Jews to that British mandate territory in the Middle East.

The end of World War II produced even more significant movements of peoples, especially on the European continent. Many of the several hundred thousand Eastern European Jews who survived the [Holocaust](/research-starters/military-history-and-science/holocaust) left Europe to migrate to Palestine even before the independent state of Israel was carved out of it in 1948. Still others emigrated there after 1948, largely stripping Eastern Europe of its once large and prosperous Jewish community. Meanwhile, under the terms of the [Potsdam Agreement](/research-starters/military-history-and-science/analysis-potsdam-agreement) signed by the principal victors in World War II in 1945, massive, largely forced migrations had already occurred within Europe, when approximately 16 million ethnic Germans were sent west from Czechoslovakia and other Eastern European countries. Millions of Poles were also driven west from what had been the eastern Kresy region of Poland, which the Soviet Union acquired as part of its 1939 non-aggression pact with Germany.

The flow of migrants within Europe was not only from east to west. During the same period, hundreds of thousands of ethnic Ukrainians, Lithuanians, Estonians, and Poles were expelled from neighboring states to the Soviet Union and parts of its rapidly forming empire in Soviet-occupied Eastern Europe. As the [Cold War](/research-starters/history/cold-war) developed between the Soviet Union and its former wartime allies in the West, other population movements occurred. When the Western Allies decided to unify their zones in occupied Germany and create the independent Federal Republic of Germany, approximately 7 million Germans fled from Soviet-controlled East Germany into what would become known as West Germany.

## Global Migration After 1950

Significant as these wartime and postwar migrations were in reshuffling the peoples of Europe, the migration that would most dramatically affect Europe and North America, and which has most profoundly broken from the pattern of previous migrations, developed elsewhere. It is the one involving the peoples who have migrated from culturally and often religiously different areas in the developing world into the economically more advanced and democratic states of Europe and North America during the second half of the twentieth century.

The origins of this development in Europe lay largely in that region’s need to rebuild after World War II, which forced much of Western Europe to import large numbers of workers from abroad to fill needed positions. Germany recruited its own "[guest workers](/research-starters/politics-and-government/guest-worker-programs)" in the then-less economically advanced states of southern Europe—most notably, secular Muslims from Turkey. France turned initially to its possessions in Africa and imported large numbers of foreign workers from Tunisia, Morocco, and Algeria. Britain looked to nations from its former empire in Asia, chiefly in India, from which former British subjects were entitled to immigrate to the United Kingdom as members of the British Commonwealth, until controversy surrounding Britain’s growing "foreign population" eventually caused Britain to pass more restrictive immigration laws.

By the 1950s, the worldwide process of decolonization of European empires was coinciding with Europe’s sustained postwar economic recovery, and its need for still more imported labor. Consequently, ever more immigrants from Europe’s former colonial world were journeying legally to European countries in pursuit of far better earnings than they could receive in their newly independent nations. This trend continued until a worldwide recession that began in 1973 resulted in most European countries restricting further entry. However, by then, workers who had been recruited as temporary employees were becoming permanent members of many European countries’ workforces.

For the most part, the more restrictive European immigration laws did not so much stop immigrant flows as increase the numbers of immigrants entering European countries illegally. Most continued to come from developing countries in Africa and Asia, and a very high percentage of these people were Muslim in their religious faiths. By the turn of the twenty-first century, it was conservatively estimated that Europe’s Muslim population numbered more than 20 million and was growing at a much faster rate than the rest of the population because of family unification programs that allowed immigrants’ spouses and children to join them. Moreover, the average sizes of Muslim families were sometimes much larger than those of families of European descent.

The population of the United States was also becoming noticeably more multicultural in the period following World War II. Many American soldiers serving in occupied Japan between 1945 and 1955 returned home with Japanese spouses. The advent of the [Korean War](/research-starters/history/korean-war) in 1950 had a similar social outcome, with many American service personnel returning home with Korean spouses. Still later, the Vietnam War that stretched from the mid-1960s to 1975 further enlarged the Asian community in the United States. After the war ended, the US Congress made room in the United States for large numbers of Vietnamese refugees whose service to the United States during the war placed their lives in danger after the fall of Saigon and the withdrawal of American forces.

However, it was the south-to-north pattern in later twentieth century immigration that had the greatest impact on American society. As a result of the steady **emigration** of peoples from Mexico and other countries in Central and South America and the Caribbean, Hispanics eclipsed African Americans as the largest minority group in the United States by the turn of the twenty-first century. Moreover, by the early years of the new millennium, the illegal Hispanic immigrant population in the United States was being conservatively estimated to be in the range of between 10 and 12 million people, making the country’s growing Hispanic population a central political issue.

This new wave of immigrants from the developing world to Europe and the United States was becoming politically controversial even before the terrorist attacks on the United States of September 11, 2001, the attack by homegrown Muslim suicide bombers on London’s public transit system in 2005, and the attack by Muslim terrorists on the railroad system of Madrid, Spain, the previous year. The presence of these foreign-born populations in the modern Western world has become more controversial over time as their numbers have continued to grow and as the security concerns of [homegrown terrorists](/research-starters/political-science/homegrown-terrorism-united-states) and insecure borders have been added to the list of concerns that their presence entails.

New theories arose to attempt to explain patterns of world migration. Many economists and sociologists viewed the late-twentieth and early-twenty first century immigration trends through the perspective of globalization and its economic repercussions. The phenomenon of immigrants coming to an economically advanced nation in order to work, often sending remittances back to their home country, can be identified throughout history, but its large-scale global implications only began to be explored in the face of the modern waves of world migration. Scholars have also attempted to examine the issue from a less Eurocentric viewpoint, discussing the perceptions of migrants from developing nations and the important differences existing at the national or even subnational level. Theories ranging from neoclassical economics to [world systems theory](/research-starters/social-sciences-and-humanities/world-systems-theory) brought new ideas to the study of world migration patterns, with most agreeing that the issue remains of critical importance to humankind politically, economically, and socially.

In 2022, the United Nations International Organization for Migration produced a report detailing the trends and key data points of global migration through 2020. The US remained a main destination for migrants in 2020, with close to 51 million foreign-born individuals residing in the country. Germany was the second most popular destination, with 16 million residents in 2020. Globally, there were an estimated 281 million migrants, or 3.6 percent of the population, living in countries that were not their own, which was more than three times the number in 1970. These individuals reside primarily in Europe, which received 86.7 percent of migrants, and Asia, which received 85.6 percent; however, the US still had more international migrants than any other individual country, and India produced the most international migrants of any country. Though COVID-19 impacted migration in terms of entry, quarantine, and other requirements, the rate of international remittances only dropped slightly from the previous year, leaving its total at $702 billion. Primarily, individuals from India, China, Mexico, the Philippines, and Egypt received the most remittance in 2020.

## Bibliography

Barkan, Elliott Robert. *From All Points: America’s Immigrant West, 1870s-1952*. Bloomington: Indiana University Press, 2007.

Brettell, Caroline, and James Frank Hollifield. *Migration Theory: Talking Across Disciplines*. 4th ed. Routledge, 2023.

Fisher, Michael Herbert. *Migration: A World History*. Oxford UP, 2014.

Hatton, Timothy J., and Jeffrey G. Williamson. *Global Migration and the World Economy: Two Centuries of Policy and Performance*. MIT Press, 2008.

McClain, Paula D., and Joseph Stewart, Jr. *“Can We All Get Along?” Racial and Ethnic Minorities in American Politics*. 7th. ed. Routledge Taylor & Francis Group, 2019.

Moch, Leslie Page. *Moving Europeans: Migration in Western Europe Since 1650*. 2nd ed. Indiana University Press, 2003.

Morris, Ian. *Why the West Rules—For Now: The Patterns of History, and What They Reveal about the Future*. Profile, 2011. Print.

Vertovec, Steven, ed. *Migration and Social Cohesion*. Cheltenham, England: Edward Elgar, 1999.

Weiner, Myron. *The Global Migration Crisis: Challenge to States and to Human Rights*. New York: HarperCollins, 1995.

“World Migration Report 2022.” *International Organization for Migration*, 2022, worldmigrationreport.iom.int/wmr-2022-interactive/. Accessed 14 Mar. 2023.

--- SOURCE 3: Trends in Migration to the U.S. ---
URL: https://www.prb.org/resources/trends-in-migration-to-the-u-s/

SUMMARY:
For its first 100 years, the United States facilitated immigration, welcoming foreigners to settle a vast country. The third wave, between 1880 and 1914, brought over 20 million European immigrants to the United States, an average of 650,000 a year at a time when the United States had 75 million residents. The United States admitted an average 250,000 immigrants a year in the 1950s, 330,000 in the 1960s, 450,000 in the 1970s, 735,000 in the 1980s, and over 1 million a year since the 1990s. Since then, the immigration debate has centered on preventing the entry of terrorists, controlling unauthorized migration, and dealing with U.S. employers who request foreigners to fill jobs.

FULL CONTENT:
PRB

Resource Library

Trends in Migration to the U.S.

Date

May 19, 2014

Author

Philip Martin

Chair of the UC Comparative Immigration and Integration Program

Population Bulletin, vol. 68, no. 2. The Global Challenge Managing Migration

Focus Area

World and U.S. Population Trends

Explore: Who Are America’s Immigrants?



Under the motto e pluribus unum (from many, one), U.S. presidents frequently remind Americans that they share the immigrant experience of beginning anew in the land of opportunity.1 Immigration is widely considered to be in the national interest, since it permits individuals to better themselves as it strengthens the United States.

For its first 100 years, the United States facilitated immigration, welcoming foreigners to settle a vast country. Beginning in the 1880s, an era of qualitative immigration restrictions began as certain types of immigrants were barred: prostitutes, workers with contracts that tied them to a particular employer for several years, and Chinese. In the 1920s, quantitative restrictions or quotas set a ceiling on the number of immigrants accepted each year.2

Immigration law changed in 1965. Qualitative and quantitative restrictions were maintained, but national origin preferences that favored the entry of Europeans were dropped. U.S. immigration policy began to favor the entry of foreigners who had U.S. relatives and foreigners requested by U.S. employers. During the 1970s, the origins of most immigrants changed from Europe to Latin America and Asia: Between 2000 and 2009 over three-fourths of the 10 million immigrants admitted were from Latin America and Asia.



U.S. immigration has occurred in waves, with peaks followed by troughs (see figure). The first wave of immigrants, mostly English-speakers from the British Isles, arrived before records were kept beginning in 1820. The second wave, dominated by Irish and German Catholics in the 1840s and 1850s, challenged the dominance of the Protestant church and led to a backlash against Catholics, defused only when the Civil War practically stopped immigration in the 1860s.

The third wave, between 1880 and 1914, brought over 20 million European immigrants to the United States, an average of 650,000 a year at a time when the United States had 75 million residents. Most southern and eastern European immigrants arriving via New York’s Ellis Island found factory jobs in Northeastern and Midwestern cities. Third-wave European immigration was slowed first by World War I and then by numerical quotas in the 1920s.

Between the 1920s and 1960s, immigration paused. Immigration was low during the Depression of the 1930s, and in some years more people left the United States than arrived. Immigration rose after World War II ended, as veterans returned with European spouses and Europeans migrated. The fourth wave began after 1965, and has been marked by rising numbers of immigrants from Latin America and Asia. The United States admitted an average 250,000 immigrants a year in the 1950s, 330,000 in the 1960s, 450,000 in the 1970s, 735,000 in the 1980s, and over 1 million a year since the 1990s.

Almost 110,000 foreigners enter the United States on a typical day. Three major entry doors exist: a front door for immigrants, a side door for temporary visitors, and a back door for the unauthorized. Almost 3,100 foreigners a day receive immigrant visas or green cards that allow them to live, work, and become naturalized U.S. citizens after five years. Over 105,000 tourist, business, and student visitors arrive; some stay only a few days, while others stay for several years. Finally, over 1,500 unauthorized foreigners a day were settling in the United States until the 2008 recession reduced their number sharply. Half of the unauthorized eluded apprehension at the Mexico-U.S. border, while the others entered legally through the side door but violated the terms of their visitor visas by working or not departing.3

During the 1990s, contentious debates arose about the relationship of immigrants and their children to the U.S. educational, welfare, and political systems; and more broadly, whether the immigration system served U.S. national interests. Since then, the immigration debate has centered on preventing the entry of terrorists, controlling unauthorized migration, and dealing with U.S. employers who request foreigners to fill jobs.

Today, unauthorized migration is the main policy concern. The number of unauthorized foreigners peaked at 12.2 million in 2007, fell by almost 1 million during the recent recession, and may have increased again with economic recovery. Debates over how to prevent unauthorized migration and deal with the unauthorized already living in the United States are polarized. Many Republicans, especially in the House of Representatives, prefer an enforcement-first approach—more agents and fences on the Mexico-U.S. border and a requirement that U.S. employers submit data on newly hired employees to prevent unauthorized workers from getting jobs. President Obama and many Democrats prefer “comprehensive immigration reform” that includes more border and interior enforcement to discourage entry and employment, but also a path to legal immigrant status.

In 2006 and 2013, the U.S. Senate approved comprehensive immigration reform bills that included a path to legalization. The Senate’s Border Security, Economic Opportunity, and Immigration Modernization Act of 2013 would:

The House approved an enforcement-first bill in 2005 and has opted for a piecemeal approach to immigration reform in 2013, with bills that increase border and interior enforcement and expand guest worker programs for farm and information technology (IT) workers.

--- SOURCE 4: Global Migration Patterns Analyzed and Explained ---
URL: https://blog.myheritage.com/2025/01/global-migration-patterns-analyzed-and-explained/

SUMMARY:
This data helps you track the origins of immigrants and their descendants in the U.S. To analyze migration patterns using census records, you can compare data across different census years to identify trends in population growth, shifts in demographics, and changes in the geographic distribution of immigrant populations. The U.S. Census records include detailed information such as age, sex, race, occupation, and place of birth, which are crucial for analyzing migration trends You can use immigration records to quantify migration flows over time and analyze trends related to specific nationalities or demographics. By correlating changes in immigration policy with shifts in the numbers or origins of migrants over time, one can assess the effectiveness and consequences of these policies on global migration trends.

Global Migration Patterns Analyzed and Explained
================================================

*    By admin 

January 20, 2025

Share

[](https://blog.myheritage.com/#facebook)[](https://blog.myheritage.com/#twitter)[](https://blog.myheritage.com/#email)[](https://blog.myheritage.com/2025/01/global-migration-patterns-analyzed-and-explained/#)

#### Share

[Messenger](https://blog.myheritage.com/#facebook_messenger)[Messenger](https://blog.myheritage.com/#facebook_messenger)

[WhatsApp](https://blog.myheritage.com/#whatsapp)[WhatsApp](https://blog.myheritage.com/#whatsapp)

[Pinterest](https://blog.myheritage.com/#pinterest)

![Image 6](https://blog.myheritage.com/wp-content/themes/myheritageredesign/img/single-copy-link.svg)Copy Link

![Image 7: Global Migration Patterns Analyzed and Explained](https://blog.myheritage.com/wp-content/uploads/A_globe_on_a_table_in_an_office-1731678495033-753x423.jpg)

![Image 8: Global Migration Patterns Analyzed and Explained](https://blog.myheritage.com/wp-content/uploads/A_globe_on_a_table_in_an_office-1731678495033-753x423.jpg)

[](https://blog.myheritage.com/#whatsapp)[](https://blog.myheritage.com/#facebook)[](https://blog.myheritage.com/2025/01/global-migration-patterns-analyzed-and-explained/#)

[](https://blog.myheritage.com/#facebook_messenger)[](https://blog.myheritage.com/#twitter)[](https://blog.myheritage.com/#email)

Today’s migration patterns have reached unprecedented levels of complexity and scale. An estimated 281 million people now live outside their countries of birth—a number larger than the population of Indonesia. [1]

Climate disasters displace another 20 million annually, while economic inequality drives millions more to seek better lives across borders. Traditional [immigration systems](https://www.myheritage.com/wiki/Immigration_to_the_U.S.), designed for a simpler era, buckle under the pressure. Host countries struggle to integrate newcomers, while sending nations grapple with brain drain and fractured families.

But within this challenge lies an opportunity to reimagine how humanity moves and adapts in an interconnected world.

» Start your family’s migration patterns with [PedigreeMap™️](https://www.myheritage.com/family-tree?tr_id=m_9k47eryygr_1yszruodtt)

Why do we have global migration?
--------------------------------

The most common reason for migration today is finding work. According to the European Parliament, around two-thirds of all [international migrants](https://www.myheritage.com/wiki/How_to_research_immigrant_ancestors) move for better job opportunities. We can categorize these forces driving migration into “push” and “pull” factors.

### Push factors

*   **Economic hardship:** Poor job opportunities and living conditions in someone’s home country can push them to seek work elsewhere.
*   **Natural disasters:** People have always fled disasters like floods or crop failures caused by climate change.
*   **Political and social issues:** Religious persecution, for example, is a historical reason for migration (e.g., [Christian colonists in New England](https://www.myheritage.com/wiki/The_New_England_Colonies)).

### Pull factors

*   **Economic opportunities:** Job openings or booming industries in another country can attract migrants. (e.g., Industrial revolutions in Europe and the US)
*   **Safety and security:** People may flee violence or war zones to find safety in another country.

» Learn to follow your [family’s immigration trail](https://education.myheritage.com/webinar/following-your-familys-immigration-trail-on-myheritage/)

Global migration patterns and their implications
------------------------------------------------

### 1. Early human migration

Early human migration refers to the movement of Homo sapiens and their ancestors from Africa to various parts of the world, primarily occurring between 100,000 and 10,000 years ago. It was driven by environmental changes, resource availability, and the search for new habitats.

Researchers suggest that this migration occurred in waves, made possible by favorable climates that created green corridors across the Arabian Peninsula and into Eurasia. These waves allowed early humans to spread into [Europe](https://www.myheritage.com/wiki/Europe_historical_records), Asia, Australia, and eventually the Americas.

As early humans migrated, they ventured into diverse environments and cultures, leading to exchanges that shaped human societies. This movement created varied languages, tools, and social structures across different regions. But it also brought extinction. The migration of Homo sapiens contributed to the disappearance of other hominid species, such as Neanderthals and Homo erectus, as they competed for resources and territory.

The spread of Homo sapiens into new areas largely altered local ecosystems. Early humans adapted their hunting and gathering practices to different environments, which sometimes led to the extinction of large mammals like mammoths due to overhunting.

[![Image 9: A map of early human migration.](https://blog.myheritage.com/wp-content/uploads/A_map_of_homo_sapiens_migration_patterns_1-1731658143624.jpg)](https://blog.myheritage.com/wp-content/uploads/A_map_of_homo_sapiens_migration_patterns_1-1731658143624.jpg "A map of early human migration.")
A map of early human migration.

» Did you know that some immigrants [changed their surname at Ellis island](https://education.myheritage.com/article/was-your-surname-really-changed-at-ellis-island/)?

### 2. **Classical and medieval migrations**

Classical and medieval migrations were significant movements of people during the classical era, approximately 500 BCE to 500 CE, and the medieval period, which occurred around 500 CE to 1500 CE. These migrations were often driven by factors such as economic opportunities, invasions, territorial expansion, and environmental changes.

For example, Germanic tribes migrated into the [Roman Empire](https://www.myheritage.com/wiki/Roman_Empire) during the late antiquity period, specifically from the 3rd to the 5th centuries CE. Groups such as the Visigoths, Ostrogoths, and Vandals moved into Roman territories, leading to significant conflicts and contributing to the fall of the Western Roman Empire.

The influx of [Germanic tribes](https://education.myheritage.com/article/researching-german-ancestors-on-myheritage/) resulted in the fragmentation of the Roman Empire and the establishment of new kingdoms in formerly Roman territories. The shift marked the transition from classical antiquity to the medieval period in Europe, leading to a feudal system.

As these tribes settled in new regions, they interacted with local populations, leading to cultural exchanges. As a result, the traditions, languages, and governance structures blended, shaping the development of medieval European culture. The migrations also disrupted trade routes and economic systems established by the Romans but also led to new economic practices as local economies adapted to new rulers and populations.

[![Image 10: A map of the early classical and medieval migration.](https://blog.myheritage.com/wp-content/uploads/Medieval_migration_patterns-1731658339395.jpg)](https://blog.myheritage.com/wp-content/uploads/Medieval_migration_patterns-1731658339395.jpg "A map of the early classical and medieval migration.")
A map of the early classical and medieval migration.

» Check out our list of [must-have genealogical databases](https://education.myheritage.com/article/9-must-have-myheritage-databases/)

### 3. Colonial era (1500s to 1800s)

The Colonial Era (1500s-1800s) saw a massive movement of people from Europe to the Americas and other colonies. This migration was driven by several factors, including economic opportunities, religious freedom, and the need for labor on plantations.

A particularly dark chapter in this era was the [transatlantic slave trade](https://www.myheritage.com/wiki/Transatlantic_Slave_Trade). Between the 16th and 19th centuries, an estimated 10 million Africans were forcibly transported to the Americas to work on plantations in the Caribbean, Brazil, and North America. These plantations produced crops like sugar, tobacco, and cotton.

The arrival of European settlers and enslaved Africans dramatically changed the demographics of the Americas. This led to a complex society with a mix of indigenous peoples, Europeans, and Africans. The diverse population influenced the region’s culture and social structures.

The plantation system, reliant on slave labor, became a major source of wealth for colonial powers. This economic model not only fueled the growth of these empires but also laid the foundation for economic inequalities that persist today. The reliance on enslaved labor created a rigid social hierarchy based on race.

» Explore the [lesser-known genealogical resources](https://education.myheritage.com/article/resources-for-genealogical-research-you-may-never-have-thought-of/)

### 4. 19th Century Migration Waves

The 19th century was a time of great change and movement, particularly for people in Europe. Millions of Europeans, especially from Ireland, Germany, and Italy, left their homelands and traveled to the Americas in search of better lives.

One of the most significant events that triggered mass migration was the [Irish Potato Famine (1845-1852)](https://www.myheritage.com/wiki/Irish_Potato_Famine). This devastating famine caused widespread hunger and disease in Ireland, leading to the deaths of over a million people. To escape the terrible conditions, another million Irish people emigrated to the U.S. Many settled in cities like New York and Boston.

The arrival of so many Irish immigrants dramatically changed the makeup of American cities. By 1860, Irish-born people made up a large part of the population in many urban areas. This influx of immigrants often caused tension with existing residents, who viewed them with suspicion and hostility. Irish immigrants, in particular, faced discrimination and prejudice.

Despite the challenges they faced, immigrants played a vital role in the American economy. They worked in factories, [built railroads](https://www.myheritage.com/wiki/The_Underground_Railroad_to_Freedom_in_the_North), and helped to grow food, contributing to the nation’s prosperity.

You can use the **MyHeritage PedigreeMap™** to visualize your family history geographically. You’ll plot the locations associated with your ancestors on a map, making it easier to identify migration patterns over generations. You can also see where your ancestors lived at different points in time and how their movements correspond with historical events or immigration waves.

Additionally, the **Heatmap feature**provides a high-level view of where your family has historically resided. By filtering data by year and type of event (births, deaths, etc.), you can observe geographic shifts in your [family’s history](https://www.myheritage.com/research). This can help identify areas with significant concentrations of ancestors, indicating potential migration routes or settlement patterns

### 5. Post-World War II Migration

The years following World War II saw a huge movement of people across Europe. Many people were looking for better lives, fleeing political unrest, or seeking refuge after the war’s devastation.

One significant example of this migration was the movement of ethnic Germans from [Eastern Europe](https://www.myheritage.com/wiki/How_to_research_ancestors_in_Eastern_Europe) back to Germany. Millions of Germans were forced to leave their homes in countries like Poland, Czechoslovakia, and Hungary due to political changes and rising tensions. This massive displacement, along with other refugee movements, reshaped the demographics of Europe.

In Germany, the return of millions of ethnic Germans led to significant population changes and challenges in rebuilding the country. Many of these returning Germans faced hostility and discrimination from local populations.

The large number of displaced people worldwide also led to the development of international laws and policies to protect refugees. These developments have shaped how countries respond to migration and asylum seekers to this day.

» Learn how the [International Refugee Organization](https://www.myheritage.com/wiki/International_Refugee_Organization) helped immigrants find homes post WWII

### 6. Contemporary Migration Trends (1970s-Present)

In recent decades, people have been moving around the world more than ever before. This movement is driven by many factors, including the search for better jobs, escaping conflict, and seeking safety from environmental disasters.

One major example of this is the migration of Venezuelans. Millions have left their country due to political and economic problems. Many Venezuelans have traveled to neighboring countries like Colombia, Peru, and Ecuador, and even further afield to the United States. This mass migration has had a big impact on [South America](https://www.myheritage.com/wiki/Latin_America_historical_records). Countries like Colombia have had to deal with the challenges of hosting so many refugees while also recognizing the benefits.

While migrants can put a strain on local resources, they also contribute to the economy by filling job vacancies. But, the arrival of large numbers of migrants can sometimes lead to tension with local communities. Governments should have good policies in place to help everyone integrate and live together peacefully.

» Learn more about [Mesoamerican and Andean ethnicity](https://www.myheritage.com/wiki/Mesoamerican_and_Andean_ethnicity)

6 resources for analyzing global migration patterns
---------------------------------------------------

### 1. Census records

[Census records](https://www.myheritage.com/research/category-1000/census-voter-lists?tr_id=m_lseblmr56u_yyk45pqmse) are official counts of a population conducted at regular intervals, typically every ten years, by governments. They provide detailed demographic information, including age, sex, race, occupation, and place of birth, which can be instrumental in understanding migration patterns.

For example, the U.S. Census of 1880 included questions about the birthplace of individuals and their parents. This data helps you track the origins of immigrants and their descendants in the U.S.

To analyze migration patterns using census records, you can compare data across different census years to identify trends in population growth, shifts in demographics, and changes in the geographic distribution of immigrant populations. For instance, you can examine the increase in foreign-born individuals in specific states or cities over time, which can help you infer the impact of immigration waves from specific countries.

[MyHeritage](https://www.myheritage.com/?utm_source=organic_blog&utm_medium=blog&utm_campaign=web&tr_funnel=web&tr_country=US&tr_creative=homepage&utm_content=homepage) has a vast collection of census and voter list records, totaling over a billion entries from various countries, including the U.S., Canada, the U.K., and many others. You can use it to trace your ancestors across multiple census years and get snapshots of demographic changes over time.

The U.S. Census records include detailed information such as age, sex, race, occupation, and place of birth, which are crucial for analyzing migration trends

### 2. Ships’ manifests

Ships’ manifests are official documents that [list all passengers aboard a vessel](https://www.myheritage.com/wiki/Passenger_lists). These records typically include details such as passenger names, ages, occupations, countries of origin, and intended destinations. They are crucial for understanding historical migration flows.

The passenger lists for ships arriving in New York from 1820 to 1957 provide extensive data on immigrants entering the U.S. For instance, the manifest for a ship arriving in 1905 might list hundreds of passengers from various European countries seeking new opportunities.

You can analyze ships’ manifests to identify specific migration routes and trends over time. By examining the countries of origin and demographic information of passengers on different voyages, you can assess how economic or political conditions in those countries influenced emigration. Additionally, notations on these manifests (e.g., reasons for detention or health issues) can provide insights into the challenges faced by migrants.

» Find your ancestors using [border crossing records](https://www.myheritage.com/wiki/Border_crossing_records)

### 3. Immigration records

[Immigration records](https://www.myheritage.com/wiki/U.S._legal_immigration_records) include documents such as visas, naturalization papers, and entry logs maintained by governments to track individuals entering a country. These records often contain personal details about migrants, including their nationality, reason for migration, and family connections.

For example, the U.S. National Archives holds immigration records from Ellis Island (1892-1954), where millions of immigrants entered the U.S. These records can include passenger lists and individual case files detailing the circumstances of each immigrant’s arrival.

You can use immigration records to quantify migration flows over time and analyze trends related to specific nationalities or demographics. You can also correlate this data with historical events (e.g., wars or economic crises) to understand how external factors influenced migration patterns.

### 4. Academic journals

Academic journals publish peer-reviewed research articles that explore various aspects of migration, including historical analyses, case studies, and theoretical frameworks. These articles often synthesize existing research and contribute new findings to the field.

For example, the “International Migration Review” or “Journal of Ethnic and Migration Studies” feature studies on contemporary migration trends, policy impacts, and immigrant integration experiences.

You can use academic journals to stay informed about recent research findings in migration studies. By reviewing literature on specific topics (e.g., refugee movements or labor migration), researchers can identify gaps in knowledge or emerging trends that warrant further investigation.

» Check out the [U.S. main points of entry for immigration](https://www.myheritage.com/wiki/U.S._ports_of_entry_for_immigration)

### 5. Government documents

Government documents related to immigration policies and regulations include laws, treaties, and reports that outline the legal framework governing migration. These documents can reveal how government actions affect migration trends.

The Immigration Act of 1924 in the U.S. established quotas that limited immigration from certain countries. Analyzing this legislation helps understand how policy changes directly impacted migration flows.

To analyze migration patterns using government documents, researchers can examine how specific laws influenced the demographics of incoming populations. By correlating changes in immigration policy with shifts in the numbers or origins of migrants over time, one can assess the effectiveness and consequences of these policies on global migration trends.

» Learn more about the [Indo-Caribbean migraton](https://www.myheritage.com/wiki/Indo-Caribbean_migration)

### 5. Historical Newspapers

[Historical newspapers](https://education.myheritage.com/downloads/42-reasons-to-use-the-myheritage-newspaper-collection/) provide contemporary accounts of migration events, public sentiment regarding immigrants, and coverage of policies affecting migration. They serve as primary sources that reflect societal attitudes and experiences during specific periods.

Articles from newspapers in the late 19th century may cover the arrival of large groups of Irish immigrants in cities like New York, discussing both the challenges they faced and the reactions from local populations.

You can analyze historical newspapers to gauge public opinion on immigration at different times and identify patterns in how migrants were portrayed. This analysis can reveal shifts in societal attitudes toward different immigrant groups and inform discussions about nationalism and identity.

Why we wander: The unfolding story of human migration
-----------------------------------------------------

Global migration, a complex phenomenon shaped by a myriad of factors, has been an integral part of human history. From the earliest human migrations out of Africa to the contemporary waves of people seeking better opportunities, safety, or refuge, the movement of people across borders has had profound impacts on societies, economies, and cultures worldwide.

Understanding the historical and contemporary drivers of migration, such as economic disparities, political instability, environmental change, and social factors, is crucial to comprehending the global landscape. By analyzing historical migration patterns and contemporary trends, we can gain insights into the challenges and opportunities associated with human mobility.

The number of people living outside their country of birth rose by 83% between 1990 and 2020, from 153 million to 281 million.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Throughout the 20th century, migration trends shifted, with significant movements from Europe to North America, particularly during the Great Migration of Black Americans seeking better opportunities.
Major events like the Irish Potato Famine and World Wars reshaped migration, prompting millions to seek refuge and better living conditions in the Americas and beyond.
Globalization has enhanced economic opportunities and interconnectedness, driving migration flows as individuals seek work in developed nations while maintaining ties to their home countries.
